content:
13 min read 3 hours ago -- Now that we have learned the basics of Docker by building a simple containerized Node.js app, let’s add a bit more complexity. The next project is called PingBoard. PingBoard will be a mini uptime monitoring app. It will let us add URLs to monitor, check whether those URLs are online or offline, store the results in PostgreSQL, and display everything on a simple dashboard in the browser. The goal is to understand Docker more deeply by building a small multi-container application from scratch. Pingboard should: • Let me add URLs to monitor • Check whether each URL is online or offline • Store URLs and check results in PostgreSQL • Show a simple dashboard in the browser • Provide a /health endpoint • Use Caddy as the reverse proxy • Use Docker Compose to run everything The Docker Architecture PingBoard will have four containers: app container → Node.js / Express web app worker container → Background process that checks URLs db container → PostgreSQL database caddy container → Reverse proxy exposed to the public internetThe app container will serve the web dashboard. This is what I interact with in the browser. The worker container won’t serve web pages. Instead, it will quietly run in the background and repeatedly check the URLs stored in the database. For example, every minute it can wake up, fetch each URL, record whether it is online or offline, and save the result. The db container will run PostgreSQL. It will store the URLs I want to monitor, as well as the history of uptime checks. The caddy container will act as the reverse proxy. It will be the only container exposed to the public internet. When someone visits the server, the request will first hit Caddy and Caddy will forward the request to the app container inside the private Docker network. The architecture will look like this: Press enter or click to view image in full size 1. Creating the Project Structure Start by creating a new folder called pingboard. mkdir pingboard cd pingboardNext, initialize the Node.js project. Inside the pingboard folder, run: npm init -yThen install the packages PingBoard will need: npm install express pg axios dotenvNow open package.json and update the scripts section: "scripts": { "start": "node src/server.js", "worker": "node src/worker.js" }The start script will run the web app: npm run startThe worker script will run the background worker: npm run workerThis is an important idea in this project. A Node.js process is just a running Node.js program. When we run: node src/server.jswe are starting one Node.js process for the web app. When we run: node src/worker.jswe are starting another Node.js process for the background worker. Later, Docker will make this separation more concrete. We will build one Docker image from the same codebase, but Docker Compose will create two different containers from that image. One container will run the web app process and another container will run the worker process. So the app and worker share the same project files, but they will be two separate containers, each with its own main process. Press enter or click to view image in full size 2. Creating the Web App Process The first file I will create is the web app. This will be the Node.js process that serves the browser dashboard and responds to HTTP requests. Inside the pingboard folder, create the src folder and the server.js file: mkdir src touch src/server.jsThen open src/server.js and add this: const express = require("express"); require("dotenv").config();const app = express(); const PORT = process.env.APP_PORT || 3000; app.use(express.urlencoded({ extended: true })); app.get("/health", (req, res) => { res.send("OK"); }); app.get("/", (req, res) => { res.send(` PingBoard

PingBoard

A mini uptime monitoring app.

`); }); app.listen(PORT, "0.0.0.0", () => { console.log(`PingBoard app is running on port ${PORT}`); }); The first line imports Express into this file. Express is the web framework we are using. Node.js by itself can create a web server but Express gives us a simpler way to define routes like /health and /. The second line loads environment variables: require("dotenv").config();This tells Node to read values from a .env file and make them available through process.env. For example, later we use: const PORT = process.env.APP_PORT || 3000;Next, we create the Express app: const app = express();The express variable is a function. When we call it with express(), it creates an Express application object. That app object is what we use to define routes and start the web server such as: app.get("/health", ...); app.get("/", ...); app.listen(...);All of these are methods on the app object. Finally, we add this line: app.use(express.urlencoded({ extended: true }));This line adds middleware to the Express app. Middleware is code that runs before the route handler. In this case, the middleware teaches Express how to read form data submitted from an HTML form. So when we say middleware runs before the route handler, we mean Express can process the request first before it reaches code like this: app.post("/monitors", (req, res) => { // handle the submitted form here });In PingBoard, we need this because our page has an HTML form:
When the user submits this form, the browser sends a POST request to /monitors. The submitted data is sent in the request body. For example, if the user types: https://example.comthe browser sends something like this: url=https%3A%2F%2Fexample.comThat format is called URL-encoded form data. By default, Express does not automatically read that form data for us. The raw data is inside the request, but Express needs to be told how to parse it. That is why we add: app.use(express.urlencoded({ extended: true }));This middleware reads the submitted form data and turns it into a JavaScript object on req.body. Because the input field has this name: name="url"Express will later let us access the submitted value like this: req.body.urlSo inside the /monitors route, we will be able to write something like: app.post("/monitors", (req, res) => { const url = req.body.url; // Later, we will save this URL into PostgreSQL. res.send(`You submitted: ${url}`); }); Without this middleware, Express would receive the form submission, but req.body would not be properly populated. In other words, this line is what makes req.body.url work. The extended: true option tells Express to use a parser that can handle slightly more complex form data. For this project, our form is simple so the difference is not very important. The important idea is that express.urlencoded(...) allows Express to understand form submissions from normal HTML forms. So this line: app.use(express.urlencoded({ extended: true }));means: Before the request reaches my route handlers, please check whether it contains URL-encoded form data. If it does, parse that data and put it into req.body. That way, when the user submits the form, our Express route can easily read the URL they typed. The /health route gives us a basic health check endpoint: app.get("/health", (req, res) => { res.send("OK"); });This is useful because later when the app is running inside Docker, we can quickly check whether the container is responding. The / route returns a basic HTML page: app.get("/", (req, res) => { res.send(` ... `); });At the bottom, the app starts listening: app.listen(PORT, "0.0.0.0", () => { console.log(`PingBoard app is running on port ${PORT}`); });As we previously explored, the "0.0.0.0" part is important for Docker. If this app only listened on localhost, it would only accept requests from inside the container itself. But when we run this app in Docker, Caddy will be in a different container and will need to forward requests to the app container. So we tell Express to listen on 0.0.0.0, which means: listen on all network interfaces inside the container. This allows another container (Caddy in our case) to reach the app. 3. Handling the Form Submission Right now, the page shows a form, but the form does not actually do anything yet. The form says:
This means that when the user submits the form, the browser will send a POST request to /monitors But in our Express app, we have not created a route handler for POST /monitors yet. At the moment, our app only has these two routes: app.get("/health", ...); app.get("/", ...);So if the user submits the form now, Express receives the request but does not know what to do with it. Let’s add a route handler for the form submission. Open src/server.js and add this route below the / route and above app.listento verify that the middleware is working properly and the form data is properly parsed. app.post("/monitors", (req, res) => { const url = req.body.url; res.send(` PingBoard

PingBoard

You submitted: ${url}

Go back `); });4. Creating the Database Schema The web app can now receive a URL from the form. At the moment, though, the URL is not saved anywhere. If I restart the app, the submitted URL disappears because it only existed inside that one HTTP request. The next step is to create the PostgreSQL tables that PingBoard will use. Inside the pingboard folder, create a new folder called db: mkdir dbThen create a file called init.sql inside it: touch db/init.sqlOpen db/init.sql and add this: CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS monitors ( id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, url TEXT NOT NULL UNIQUE, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW () );CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS checks ( id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, monitor_id INTEGER NOT NULL REFERENCES monitors (id) ON DELETE CASCADE, status TEXT NOT NULL, status_code INTEGER, response_time_ms INTEGER, error TEXT, checked_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW () ); PingBoard needs two tables because it stores two different kinds of data. The first table is monitors. CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS monitors ( id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, url TEXT NOT NULL UNIQUE, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW () );This table stores the URLs I want to monitor. For example, if I add two URLs, the table could look like this: id | url | created_at ---|---------------------|------------------------- 1 | https://example.com | 2026-06-27 10:00:00+00 2 | https://google.com | 2026-06-27 10:01:00+00Each monitor has an id. This is the unique identifier for that URL. The url column stores the actual URL. It is marked as UNIQUE, which means PostgreSQL will not allow the same URL to be added twice. The created_at column records when the monitor was created. The second table is checks. CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS checks ( id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, monitor_id INTEGER NOT NULL REFERENCES monitors (id) ON DELETE CASCADE, status TEXT NOT NULL, status_code INTEGER, response_time_ms INTEGER, error TEXT, checked_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW () );This table stores the results of each uptime check. For example, if PingBoard checks both https://example.com and https://google.com four times, the checks table could contain rows like this: | id | monitor_id | status | status_code | response_time_ms | error | checked_at | | -- | ---------- | ------- | ----------- | ---------------- | --------------------- | ---------------------- | | 1 | 1 | online | 200 | 153 | null | 2026-06-27 10:00:00+00 | | 2 | 1 | online | 301 | 162 | null | 2026-06-27 10:01:00+00 | | 3 | 1 | offline | 500 | 241 | null | 2026-06-27 10:02:00+00 | | 4 | 1 | offline | null | null | timeout | 2026-06-27 10:03:00+00 | | 5 | 2 | online | 204 | 98 | null | 2026-06-27 10:00:30+00 | | 6 | 2 | offline | 404 |
